title: Math 314: Practice Final Exam (Full)
description: Full conversion of the May 2026 practice final covering the entirety of Linear Algebra coursework.

questions.prompt: True or False: If $A$ and $B$ are row-equivalent matrices then we always have $\text{row}(A)=\text{row}(B)$[cite: 5].
questions.explanation: Row operations are linear combinations of rows; therefore, the span of the rows (the row space) remains unchanged[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: True or False: A system of 3 equations in 2 unknowns must be inconsistent[cite: 5].

choices.label: False — it could have a unique solution or infinitely many if equations are redundant.

questions.explanation: While it can be inconsistent, it is not mandatory. For example, if all three equations represent the same line, there are infinitely many solutions[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: True or False: For any matrix $A$, $(\text{row}(A))^{\perp}=\text{null}(A)$[cite: 5].
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra states that the null space is the orthogonal complement of the row space[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: True or False: If a $4\times7$ matrix $A$ has 4 pivot columns, then $\text{col}(A)=\mathbb{R}^{4}$[cite: 5].

choices.label: True — 4 linearly independent vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$ span $\mathbb{R}^4$.

questions.explanation: Since the column space is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^4$ and its dimension (rank) is 4, it must be the entire space[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Consider a $6\times6$ matrix $A$ having $\text{dim}(\text{null}(A))=1$. What is the dimension of $\text{col}(A)$?[cite: 5]
questions.explanation: Rank + Nullity = $n$. Here, $rank + 1 = 6$, so rank (the dimension of the column space) is 5[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: What is the smallest possible dimension for $\text{null}(B)$ if $B$ is a $10\times6$ matrix?[cite: 5]

choices.label: 0 — if the columns are linearly independent.

questions.explanation: A $10\times6$ matrix has 6 columns. If all 6 columns are pivots, the rank is 6, making the nullity $6 - 6 = 0$[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Is a $3\times3$ matrix $C$ with 3 distinct eigenvalues diagonalizable?[cite: 5]

choices.label: Yes — any $n\times n$ matrix with $n$ distinct eigenvalues is diagonalizable.

questions.explanation: Distinct eigenvalues guarantee linearly independent eigenvectors, which provide the basis for diagonalization[cite: 5].
Expanding along the bottom row (which has three zeros) gives $2 \times \det(\text{submatrix})$. Further expansion yields a final result of 30[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Find all values of $a$ and $b$ for which the system $\begin{cases} x_1 + ax_2 = 2 \\ 4x_1 + 8x_2 = b \end{cases}$ is inconsistent[cite: 5].
questions.explanation: The second equation is $4(x_1 + 2x_2) = b$. If $a=2$, the left sides are multiples. If $b \neq 8$, the equations contradict each other[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Let $W = \text{span} \{ \mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \mathbf{u}_3 \}$ where $\mathbf{u}_1=[1,1,1,1]^T$, $\mathbf{u}_2=[1,-1,-1,1]^T$, and $\mathbf{u}_3=[1,1,-1,-1]^T$. Is this an orthogonal set?[cite: 5]

choices.label: Yes — the dot product of every pair is zero.

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2 = 1-1-1+1=0$; $\mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_3 = 1+1-1-1=0$; $\mathbf{u}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_3 = 1-1+1-1=0$. Since all pairs are orthogonal, the set is orthogonal[cite: 5].
